skill.name: request-refactor-plan
skill.description: Create a detailed refactor plan with tiny commits via user interview, then file it as a GitHub issue. Use when user wants to plan a refactor, create a refactoring RFC, or break a refactor into safe incremental steps.
skill.summary: Create a detailed refactor plan with tiny commits via user interview, then file it as a GitHub issue...
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported five high-severity weak cryptographic algorithm findings in SKILL.md. Human review found no cryptographic APIs, algorithms, scripts, network calls, secret access, prompt injection, or malicious behavior; all five static findings are false positives from ordinary prose.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 3 appears in the frontmatter description. It describes refactor planning and GitHub issue creation, not cryptographic behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line contains a skill description only. No weak cryptographic algorithm, crypto library, hashing call, or encryption instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 8 is an instruction to ask the user for refactor context. It does not mention cryptography or unsafe algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is plain workflow guidance for gathering requirements. There is no executable content or cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 22 introduces a GitHub issue template. It does not request encryption, hashing, or weak crypto usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line directs issue creation using a template. It has no suspicious code pattern, external command, or crypto reference.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 56 is testing guidance about external behavior. It is unrelated to cryptography or security-sensitive execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line discusses test quality. No code execution, crypto API, or unsafe algorithm appears in context.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md line 62 asks for out-of-scope items in a refactor plan. It is not a cryptographic instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is normal planning text. It contains no reference to weak algorithms, sensitive data, or malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Large refactors can fail when scope, testing, and commit order are unclear. This skill guides an interview and turns decisions into a GitHub issue plan.
actual_capabilities: Interviews the user about the refactor problem, solution ideas, and scope.
Explores the repository to verify assumptions before writing a plan.
Breaks a refactor into small commits that should keep the project working.
Documents implementation decisions, testing decisions, and out-of-scope items.
Formats the result as a GitHub issue description for team review.
limitations: It creates a plan, not the refactor implementation.
It depends on repository access to verify codebase claims.
It may need user input for unclear architecture or testing goals.
GitHub issue creation requires an available GitHub workflow or tool.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Legacy Refactor
use_cases.description: Turn a broad modernization goal into scoped phases, testing decisions, and small reviewable commits.

use_cases.title: Create a Refactoring RFC
use_cases.description: Capture tradeoffs, constraints, and implementation decisions before a team commits to a refactor.

use_cases.title: Plan Safe Contributor Work
use_cases.description: Help a contributor understand boundaries, expected tests, and commit order before changing shared code.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a refactor discussion from a rough idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a refactor plan for this repository. Interview me first, then inspect the code and propose small commits.

prompt_templates.title: Scope a Known Problem
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert a known pain point into an actionable plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to refactor this area because it is hard to maintain. Ask clarifying questions, verify the current structure, and define scope.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Team RFC
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a planning issue for review before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a refactoring RFC as a GitHub issue. Include problem, solution, tiny commits, decisions, tests, and out-of-scope items.

prompt_templates.title: Stress Test a Refactor Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate alternatives before committing to a major design change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Challenge my proposed refactor, compare alternatives, inspect the existing tests, and produce a minimal-risk plan for review.

output_examples.input: Plan a refactor that separates billing logic from the user service.
output_examples.output: A GitHub issue draft with a problem statement, proposed solution, small commit sequence, decision log, testing plan, and out-of-scope list.

output_examples.input: Create an RFC for replacing a fragile data access layer.
output_examples.output: A structured refactor request that records repository findings, alternatives considered, module boundaries, test coverage gaps, and phased implementation steps.

output_examples.input: Help a new contributor plan cleanup work without breaking behavior.
output_examples.output: A contributor-ready plan that defines safe changes, required tests, excluded work, and reviewable commit boundaries.

best_practices: Provide the business problem, constraints, and any attempted solutions before planning starts.
Let the assistant inspect related code and tests before finalizing scope.
Review the generated GitHub issue with teammates before implementation begins.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to skip design discussion for high-risk refactors.
Do not accept a plan that was written without checking the repository.
Do not include brittle file paths or code snippets in long-lived issue text.

faq.question: Does this skill implement the refactor?
faq.answer: No. It creates a detailed plan and issue description for later implementation.

faq.question: Can it create a GitHub issue?
faq.answer: Yes, when the environment provides a suitable GitHub workflow or tool.

faq.question: Why does it ask many questions first?
faq.answer: Refactors need clear scope, constraints, and testing decisions before commit planning is useful.

faq.question: Does it inspect the repository?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill asks the assistant to verify user claims against the current codebase.

faq.question: What should the final plan include?
faq.answer: It should include the problem, solution, commits, decisions, tests, out-of-scope items, and optional notes.

faq.question: Is this useful for small cleanups?
faq.answer: It is best for refactors that need team alignment or careful sequencing.
